evidence_quotes.quote: NSFW games from Steam and Itch.io being removed due to the demands of credit card companies.
to target queer representation in video games, such as the representation of transgender individuals.

paragraphs.text: https://www.polygon.com/steam-visa-mastercard-paypal-censorship-project-2025-early-access/ Credit card companies are not the arbiters of moral authority, and should have no say in what users are allowed to buy using their services so long as these services are legal in nature. I propose this be written into law to prevent situations like we have seen earlier this year regarding many NSFW games from Steam and Itch.io being removed due to the demands of credit card companies. This is a dangerous precedent to be set and only allows bad actors such as Collective Shout to obtain disproportionate power over what subjects are deemed "acceptable" for people to enjoy regarding the media they consume, further increasing censorship across the internet and increasing the risk that marginalized groups will have their stories and identities censored and silenced. While I personally have no problem with many of the games listed on Steam and Itch.io being removed in this situation, as I find many of these games to be disgusting, I recognize that it would be extremely easy for groups like Collective Action to misuse the same rhetoric they used in this case to target queer representation in video games, such as the representation of transgender individuals. Collective Action themselves seem to be very open about wishing to censor queer expression. Because credit card companies like Visa and Mastercard have this much disproportionate influence over platforms, small niche groups like this can have immense impact on the free expression of many marginalized groups. Even if bad actors were not blatantly abusing the power these companies have to push their own agendas, the very fact that these companies have this much power is extremely concerning. Once again, credit card companies should not be the arbiters of what media is moral or immoral to consume. While what is legal is not necessarily moral, I personally trust laws passed by the EU and other democratic nations who are more transparent and accountable to the public at large to weigh in on what media should be consumed. Thus, I propose that laws be passed making it illegal for credit card companies to forbit the purchase of certain items so long as these items are legal in their respective juristictions.
